title: Design Netflix/Video Streaming Platform
shortDescription: Design a video streaming platform like Netflix that supports video playback across multiple devices (mobile, web, smart TVs) with seamless resume functionality, subscription management, personalized recommendations, and content upload capabilities.

Were You Asked This?
Design Netflix/Video Streaming Platform

Design a video streaming platform like Netflix that supports video playback across multiple devices (mobile, web, smart TVs) with seamless resume functionality, subscription management, personalized recommendations, and content upload capabilities.

Asked at:

HubSpot

MongoDB

Meta

Google

+1 More

Netflix is an on‑demand video streaming platform where users browse a personalized homepage, press play on movies or shows, and watch seamlessly on phones, TVs, and the web. Behind the scenes it ingests large video files, transcodes them into multiple qualities, serves them via CDNs, and learns from watch behavior to power recommendations.

Interviewers ask this question to see if you can connect offline pipelines (upload/transcode), online low‑latency serving (manifests, ABR, CDNs), cross‑device state (resume points), subscriptions, and recommendations into a coherent, scalable design. They’re testing your ability to prioritize requirements, reason about trade‑offs, and use industry patterns for high read volume, large blob delivery, metrics collection, and ML‑driven ranking.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to upload long‑form videos with metadata and have them processed for reliable streaming across devices.

Users should be able to stream videos with adaptive bitrate, with start, pause, and resume from the exact timestamp on any client.

Users should be able to discover a personalized homepage of recommended movies and shows tailored to their viewing history and preferences.

Users should be able to manage a subscription that gates playback access across multiple clients (mobile, smart TVs, web).

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you process and package videos to support adaptive bitrate streaming and fast start-up?

How will you reliably capture and sync watch progress across devices without overwhelming the database?

How do you collect watch metrics (views, watch time, quartiles) at scale and make them available for analytics and ranking?

How would you serve a personalized recommendations homepage under tight latency budgets across regions?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

Videos are massive binary objects that must be stored in object storage, segmented for HLS/DASH, and distributed via CDNs. This pattern clarifies keeping blobs out of transactional stores, using range requests, and optimizing segment size for startup and rebuffering.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Transcoding, packaging, thumbnail generation, and DRM wrapping are CPU-intensive, multi-step jobs that may take minutes to hours. A workflow/saga approach with queues, retries, and checkpoints ensures resilience and observability.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Playback, manifests, artwork, and the recommendations homepage are read-dominant at massive scale. Caching, CDNs, precomputed recommendation rows, and region-local serving are essential to meet strict latency SLAs.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides durable, high-throughput ingestion for playback telemetry, upload lifecycle events, and recommendation signals. It decouples producers from consumers (transcoding orchestrator, metrics pipelines, recs updaters) and supports replay and backpressure during spikes.

Read More
Redis

Redis is ideal for low-latency state such as watch progress checkpoints, rate-limiting tokens, session data, and caching recommendation rows/manifests. TTLs and atomic operations help control write amplification and enforce SLAs.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra fits high-write, high-availability workloads for time-series watch events, per-user watch history, and resume offsets with predictable latency and multi-region replication, making it suitable for global streaming services.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

YouTube

Both require upload pipelines, multi-rendition transcoding, CDN-backed ABR streaming, watch metrics ingestion, and personalized recommendations at scale.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

The recommendations homepage mirrors a feed: precompute candidates, re-rank on read, cache aggressively, and enforce low tail latency while handling personalization and freshness.

Read
Practice
YouTube Top K

Trending rows and carousels rely on aggregating watch events and computing top-K titles over sliding windows, with deduplication and hotspot mitigation during spikes.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Streaming monolithic MP4 files directly from application servers or object storage without ABR and CDNs

This leads to high startup latency, poor resilience to network variability, cache misses, and unsustainable egress cost. Without HLS/DASH segmentation and CDN distribution, the service won’t scale.

Writing watch progress every second to a single hot row

It creates severe write amplification and hot partitions during popular releases. Without batching, rate limiting, and idempotent upserts, you risk data corruption and cascading failures.

Running heavy ML models synchronously on the recommendations read path

It blows tail latency and makes the homepage unreliable. You need offline candidate generation, lightweight online re-ranking, and caching with graceful degradation to meet SLAs.
